coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified BiOBr microspheres with enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Zhiyu
Yang, Jun
Zhou, Chuxin
Mo, Qiaofei
Zhang, Yuanming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified BiOBr (CQDs/BiOBr) microspheres were successfully prepared via a polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) assisted hydrothermal method. The obtained CQDs/BiOBr photocatalysts exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than bare BiOBr and commercial Degussa P25 in photo-degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. The experimental results indicate that the modification of CQDs can effectively enhance the concentration and the transfer rate of the photogenerated carriers, which is mainly attributed to the superior electron transfer ability and enhanced light harvesting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
BiOBr
Visible light
Photocatalysis
Rhodamine B
Ciprofloxacin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of bare BiOBr and CQDs/BiOBr with different CQDs contents; (b) SEM image; (c) TEM image and (d), (e) HRTEM image of 5% CQDs/BiOBr; (f) HRTEM image of single CQD embedded in BiOBr.
Photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples for degradation of (a) RhB and (b) CIP.

(a) Electrochemical impedance spectra and (b) PL spectra of bare BiOBr and 5% CQDs/BiOBr; (c) trapping experiments of active species during the photocatalytic reaction; (d) schematic of the separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers in the CQDs/BiOBr system.
Recycling tests of 5% CQDs/BiOBr for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified BiOBr microspheres with enhanced visible light photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified BiOBr (CQDs/BiOBr) microspheres were successfully prepared via a polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) assisted hydrothermal method. The obtained CQDs/BiOBr photocatalysts exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than bare BiOBr and commercial Degussa P25 in photo-degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP) under visible light irradiation. The experimental results indicate that the modification of CQDs can effectively enhance the concentration and the transfer rate of the photogenerated carriers, which is mainly attributed to the superior electron transfer ability and enhanced light harvesting.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the issues of energy shortages and environmental protection have aroused much attention from the general public [1]. In this regard, semiconductor photocatalysis is considered to be one of the most important solutions to these problems, which can effectively utilize the solar energy to generate hydrogen from water, to decompose the harmful waste into harmless substances, and to transform CO2 into fuel and valuable chemical feedstocks [2]. Hence, development of highly efficient photocatalysts for the organic pollutants elimination has attracted many interests and numerous efforts have been devoted to exploring highly active visible light-induced photocatalysts.

Bismuth oxyhalides BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I), as one category of promising layered Bi-based semiconductors for photocatalytic energy conversion and environment remediation, have drawn more and more attention in recent several decades [3] due to their remarkable optical and electrical properties, and their excellent photocatalytic efficiency [4]. It was reported that the internal electrostatic fields [5] existed between the positive [Bi2O2]2+ slabs [6] and double halogen atom slabs in the layered structures of BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I), can effectively separate the photo-generated electron-hole pairs [7]. They have been researched intensively because of their potential applications in pharmaceuticals [8], pigments [9] and catalysts [10], and gas sensors as well [11]. Among these BiOX photocatalysts, BiOBr has been greatly investigated owing to its appropriate band gap and excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation [12]. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers limits its photocatalytic efficiency [13]. In order to further improve the photocatalytic activity of BiOBr, considerable efforts have been made, such as microstructure modulation [14], hybridization with other materials [15], and so on.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel metal-free carbonaceous materials with sizes below 10nm [16], have found wide use in more and more fields during the last few years due to their many novel and unique properties, e.g. low cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, ease of functionalization and excellent light harvesting [17]. Especially, the superior electron transfer ability [18], electron reservoir properties [19], and good up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior [20], would improve the photocatalytic properties of CQDs-based photocatalysts [21,22]. Previous studies have shown that the electron-accepting and transport properties of carbon nanomaterials provide a convenient way to separate photogenerated electrons. Chen et al. [17] synthesized CQD modified BiOI microsphere via a facile hydrothermal process. The obtained CQD/BiOI exhibited significantly enhanced photoactivity, which was 25 times that of the pure BIOI. Ding et al. [20] have reported that ZnO foam/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite had excellent photocatalytic activities for the degradation of rhodamine B, methyl orange and methylene blue. Ye utilized the CQDs as a visible light sensitizer to boost the solar water splitting performance of NiOOH/FeOOH/CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode [22].

In this work, we synthesized CQDs/BiOBr microspheres by a PVP-assisted hydrothermal method (this section is given in the Supporting information) and evaluated the photoactivity of these catalysts for degradation of organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation. The experimental results indicate that the modification of CQDs can effectively enhance the concentration and the transfer rate of the photogenerated carriers, leading to significantly improved photoactivity and stability.

The crystal structures and phase analysis of samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) in the range of 10–80 (2θ) using a PANalytical X'Pert Pro X-ray diffractometer with 0.154nm Cu Kα radiation as light source. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Zeiss ULTRA 55) and JEOL-2010F (JEOL, Japan), respectively. Surface areas of the samples were measured by low-temperature N2 adsorption with Micromeritics TriStar 3000. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were detected on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were carried out in a conventional three-electrode system with a 0.1M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution by using an electrochemical system (SP-150, France).

The CQDs/BiOBr microspheres were synthesized by a polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) assisted hydrothermal method. The XRD patterns of bare BiOBr and CQDs/BiOBr with different CQDs contents are displayed in Fig. 1a. The pattern matches well with tetragonal phase BiOBr (JCPDS card No. 09-0393). No peaks of any other phases or impurities were observed, which confirmed the formation of pure compound. CQDs/BiOBr microspheres displayed only the characteristic peaks of BiOBr, which may be due to the low amount and small particle size of CQDs [25]. As shown in Fig. 1b and c, the as-prepared 5% CQDs/BiOBr (mass percentage) were spherical hierarchical morphologies constructed by numerous two dimensional interfaced nanoplates. The diameters of the microspheres are in the range of 0.4–1.2μm. In the high-temperature aqueous solutions at high vapor pressures, the nanoplates of BiOBr and CQDs would self-assemble into the CQDs/BiOBr microspheres due to the minimization of surface energy and the effect of PVP. The formation mechanism of CQDs/BiOBr microspheres might be divided into three main stages: (a) the formation of BiOBr nanoplates; (b) the self-assembly process of BiOBr nanoplates to form the mature microspheres; (c) the formation of carbon quantum dots modified BiOBr microspheres [23,24]. HRTEM image (Fig. 1e) of the CQDs/BiOBr microspheres reveal that numerous CQDs with the diameter about 4–6nm randomly were decorated on the surface of BiOBr nanosheets, indicating that the CQDs have been coupled with the BiOBr in the nanoscale successfully. The lattice spacing of 0.196nm and 0.282nm correspond to (200) and (102) planes of BiOBr, respectively (Fig. 1d). Fig. 1f reveals that the measured lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.202nm, corresponding to the (101) plane of graphic carbon [22].

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples and the commercial Degussa P25 were evaluated for the degradation of RhB (Fig. 2a) and CIP (Fig. 2b) under visible light irradiation. The results showed that CQDs/BiOBr microspheres exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than both bare BiOBr nanosheets and Degussa P25. Among these CQDs/BiOBr microspheres with different CQDs contents, 5% CQDs/BiOBr displayed the highest photocatalytic activity. After 30min visible-light irradiation, 94.3% of RhB was photodegraded, while only 83.7% and 18.9% of RhB were photodegraded in the presence of bare BiOBr nanosheets and Degussa P25, respectively. The BET surface area of CQDs modified BiOBr is 41.5m2/g (Fig. S1), which is 16 times than that of bare BiOBr (2.6m2/g). It has been widely acknowledged that the adsorption capacity of catalysts plays an important role in their practical applications in the treatment of wastewater containing trace amounts of organic contaminants [26]. The enhanced BET surface area of CQDs/BiOBr can facilitate its adsorption ability, leads to an increment of pollutant around the catalyst and an improvement in the rates of photocatalytic reactions [26,27]. The CQDs/BiOBr microspheres also exhibited high photocatalytic activity for the degradation of CIP, a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent that widely used for treating bacterial infections. The presence of antibacterial agents in natural environments has raised several human health concerns [28]. As shown in Fig. 2b, 5% CQDs/BiOBr exhibited the highest photoactivity for CIP removal. After irradiation for 180min, about 65.8% of CIP was removed by 5% CQDs/BiOBr microspheres, while only 43.6% and 40.7% of CIP were removed by bare BiOBr nanasheets and Degussa P25, respectively.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), a powerful diagnostic technique for the research and development of new materials and electrode structures, was used to investigate the photogenerated charge separation process. As shown in Fig. 3a, the arc radius in the EIS Nynquist plots of 5% CQDs/BiOBr was obviously smaller than that of BiOBr. This implies that 5% CQDs/BiOBr had a lower interfacial layer resistance than bare BiOBr, resulting in enhanced transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [29]. Photoluminescence (PL) technique was used to study the recombination efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. It is well known that stronger intensity represents higher efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers recombination [30,31]. As shown in Fig. 3b, 5% CQDs/BiOBr microspheres display lower PL intensity than bare BiOBr, suggesting the recombination rate of photo-generated charge carriers can be effectively suppressed after the modification of CQDs.

It is vitally important to identify the main active species in the photocatalytic reaction process to better understand the photocatalytic mechanism. By adding various scavengers, e.g. triethanolamine (TEOA) (hole scavenger) and isopropanol (IPA) (·OH scavenger), active species conducive to the oxidation process during the photocatalytic reaction of 5% CQDs/BiOBr were studied and the results are shown in Fig. 3c. The photocatalytic activity was almost completely restrained after the injection of 1mM TEOA, which meant that the h+ played an important role in the degradation. Conversely, the degradation ratio of RhB had almost no change when 1mM IPA was added, which meant very few OH were involved in the degradation of RhB. However, the photodegradation ratio of RhB decreased slightly with the addition of AgNO3 and BQ, respectively, indicating that O2
− and e− also contributed to the degradation of RhB. Based on the above results, a possible mechanism was proposed [32] (Fig. 3d). When a semiconductor catalyst absorbs light whose energy is equal to or greater than its band-gap energy, which cause the electrons to excite from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving positive holes in the VB. When CQDs/BiOBr microspheres are irradiated by visible light, the electrons of BiOBr can be excited from the VB to the CB. The photogenerated electrons on the surface of BiOBr can transfer easily to CQDs, leading to efficient separation of electron–hole pairs. On the other hand, it can greatly improve the generation numbers of electrons-hole pairs by the up-converted PL property of the CQDs on the surface of BiOBr. The CQDs could absorb long wavelength light and then emit shorter wavelength in turn to excite BiOBr. Then, the photoexcited e− can be captured by O2 molecules to generate the O2
− radicals and the holes (h+) on the VB of BiOBr could degrade pollutant effectively.

The reusability of 5% CQDs-BiOBr microspheres were tested under visible light irradiation and results are shown in Fig. 4. After six cycling tests, no obvious change in the photoactivity was observed, indicating that 5% CQDs/BiOBr microspheres were highly stable during the photocatalysis process (Fig. 4 ).

In summary, CQDs/BiOBr microspheres were successfully prepared via a PVP-assisted hydrothermal method. The CQDs-BiOBr samples exhibited significantly enhanced visible-light induced photoactivity in degradation of RhB and CIP, which is mainly attributed to the improved separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and enhanced light harvesting. After six recycles, CQDs-BiOBr microspheres did not exhibit any significant loss of activity, confirming the photocatalyst is essentially stable. This work provides useful information for the development of CQDs-based photocatalytic hybrid materials with high activity and excellent recyclability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant No. 21276104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2018.02.013.
